data.title: Ringgit Likely Rangebound Amid Mixed Macro Backdrop — Market Talk
data.description: The Malaysian ringgit is expected to be rangebound next week, trading at 4.43 to 4.47 against the U.S. dollar, Kenanga IB economists say in a note. Optimism over the economic outlook for China and Europe may weigh on the dollar index, while a higher-for-longer Fed stance and a cautious BOJ could support a rebound in USD, they say. Political risks are likely to persist in the U.S., and markets will be eyeing guidance from major central banks next week, they say. Kenanga pegs USD/MYR's short-term resistance at 4.455 and support at 4.424. USD/MYR is 0.2% higher at 4.445. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

data.title: Vruddhi Engineering Works Appoints CFO
data.description: Vruddhi Engineering Works has appointed Anushri Manoj Gurav as chief financial officer of the company with effect from Friday, according to a same-day disclosure to the BSE.

data.title: Nikkei Stock Average Extends Gains, Now Up 0.9% at 37117.75
Resource Mining Begins Exploration at Saudi Arabia Gold Projects, Shares Fall 11%
data.description:
Resource Mining said its geology team is conducting a site visit to the Wadi Salamah and Shaib Marqan gold projects in Saudi Arabia, according to a Friday Australian bourse filing.

The team will conduct mapping and rock chip sampling programs to support future exploration activities.

The firm's shares fell 11% in recent trading on Friday.

data.title: Singapore Post Invests SG$30 Million to Triple E-Commerce Parcel Sorting Capacity
data.description:
Singapore Post announced a SG$30 million investment in its regional e-commerce logistics hub facility, LogHub, to increase its parcel processing capabilities, according to a Thursday filing with the Singapore Exchange.

The capital injection will be used to install new sorting equipment designed to revolutionize the hub's operational efficiency.

Notably, the new technology will enable LogHub to process up to 300,000 small parcels per day, a threefold increase from its current capacity of 100,000 parcels.

The investment will be utilized to install new sorting equipment, which will take small parcels sorting to up to 300,000 small parcels per day, compared to 100,000 currently.

The investment is the first major renovation of the LogHub which was launched in back in 2016.

data.title: Goodwill Entertainment, Subsidiary Sign Subscription Deal with Four Investors
data.description:
Goodwill Entertainment and its wholly owned subsidiary, Yakitori One, entered into a subscription and shareholders agreement with four investors, according to a filing with the Singapore Exchange on Thursday.

Under the deal, the investors will subscribe to 480,000 new redeemable non-convertible preference shares in the issued and paid-up share capital of YO. Another 200,000 shares will be subscribed to by the investors for SG$20,000.

The proposed subscription will allow the group to raise SG$500,000 which will be utilized for the repayment of a shareholder's loan provided by the company to YO and for the subsidiary's general working capital requirements.

data.title: New Zealand Dollar Rises After Sharp Fall
data.description:
The New Zealand dollar rose to around $0.57 on Friday, recouping some losses after falling 0.5% in the previous session on concerns over an escalating global trade war.

US President Donald Trump reaffirmed his stance on implementing reciprocal tariffs on global trading partners, set to take effect on April 2.

Meanwhile, softer-than-expected inflation figures in the US reinforced bets on further Federal Reserve interest rate cuts.

Domestically, the Kiwi found support from strong manufacturing PMI data.

New Zealand's manufacturing activity in February rose to its highest level since August 2022, driven by increased production and new orders.

Meanwhile, the country’s annual food inflation inched up to 2.4% in February from 2.3% in the previous month.

data.title: Tin prices soar on mine closure (and one ASX miner that's leveraged to higher prices)
data.description:
Key points:
  • Tin prices hit an eight-month high of US$36,465/tonne after Alphamin Resources halted its Bisie mine, tightening global supply amid rebel unrest in the DRC
  • Metals X shares surged 21% to a three-year high, leveraging its Renison Tin Operation, which reported a record 11,006 tonnes and $101.8 million profit in 2024
  • Supply shocks drive short-term price spikes, but history (e.g., South32’s manganese) suggests gains may fade if Alphamin resumes, challenging Metals X’s momentum

Tin prices spiked to a fresh eight-month high after one of the world's largest miners – Alphamin Resources – announced a temporary shutdown of its Bisie mine in the Democratic Republic of the Congo due to advancing rebel forces nearing the site.

Prices on the London Metal Exchange (LME) soared as much as 9% to US$36,465 a tonne on Friday.

Alphamin produced more than 17,000 tonnes of tin last year, accounting for approximately 6% of global supply, according to the International Tin Organisation.

The company is evacuating all operational mining personnel from the site, with only essential personnel remaining for the care, maintenance, and security of the property.

Alphamin’s Toronto-listed shares plummeted 38% on Thursday to their lowest since February 2021, while the resulting supply crunch propelled Australian tin miner Metals X up 21% on Friday, hitting a near three-year high.

Metals X five-year price chart (Source: Market Index)

Metals X in a nutshell

Metals X stands out as one of the few pure-play tin producers on the ASX, operating its 50%-owned Renison Tin Operation on Tasmania’s West Coast. Here are some standout highlights from the 2024 calendar year.

  • Produced a record 11,006 tonnes (100% basis)

  • This follows a strong 2023, where production increases were driven by higher ore grades and improved mill recovery rates

  • Net profit after tax up 121% to $101.8 million, reflecting higher tin prices and a lower Australian dollar

  • Cash balance of $220.6 million as at 31 December 2024 (vs. current market cap of approximately $460 million)

  • Approved extended on-market buy-back of up to 10% of issued capital

In a nutshell, Metals X is a highly cash generative miner that's benefiting from a solid operational performance and strong underlying tin prices.

What's next?

The scenario echoes a past commodity crunch involving manganese. Here’s a quick recap:

  • What happened: South32 suspended operations at its Groote Eylandt Mining Company (GEMCO) after key infrastructure was destroyed by Tropical Cyclone Megan

  • What was the significance: The GEMCO operation supplies approximately 10% of the global manganese market, with a prior guidance for FY24 of 1.7 million tonnes

  • Stock in focus: Jupiter Mines is one of the largest manganese miners on the ASX, with a 49.9% ownership of the Tshipi Project in South Africa. Tshipi is currently a top-five global producer of manganese, with stable production, comparatively low costs, and a mine life of more than 100 years

  • Share price reaction: JMS shares rallied 10.2% on the day of the news (21 March 2024) and another 9.3% in the next session. After moving sideways for around two weeks, the stock continued to aggressively trend higher. From 21 March to its 13 May peak, the stock soared 89%

When a supply shock jolts a commodity market, the affected resource typically undergoes a sharp and rapid price surge. This escalation is fueled by a scramble among customers to lock in inventory and heightened pressure from capital markets pushing prices upward.

In the short-to-medium term, two key outcomes tend to unfold:

  • The lost supply eventually comes back online (as is the case with South32)

  • The skyrocketing commodity price sparks a robust supply response as producers rush to capitalise on the opportunity

When South32's GEMCO operation resumed production, JMS shares gradually retreated from highs of 37 cents to pre-disruption levels of around 16 cents.

Jupiter Mines 12-month price chart (Source: Market Index)

In a way, the lithium sector feels like a prolonged echo of this pattern, with miners riding a two-year bull market wave from 2021 to 2023, only to now navigate an ongoing bear market that continues to unfold.

The bottom line

Alphamin's recent mine closure has intensified the existing tin supply deficit, with Macquarie analysts predicting a global shortfall of approximately 13,000 tonnes this year.

The International Tin Association has also highlighted potential long-term supply challenges. Without additional investments in mining operations, the association forecasts a supply deficit of 13,000 tonnes by 2030, driven by increasing demand from sectors like green energy and artificial intelligence.

Metals X is already highly cash-generative, and the recent surge in tin prices has added further momentum to the stock. The key question is whether it can sustain these gains if Alphamin resumes production. Could this be a repeat of what happened with South32 and Jupiter Mines?

data.title: Natural Cool Holdings Increases Interest in iFocus
data.description:
Natural Cool Holdings increased its equity interest in iFocus to 80% following the completion of the first tranche of its investment in the subsidiary, according to a Thursday filing with the Singapore Exchange.

The second tranche will be effected on or before Aug. 31.

In an earlier filing, the company had agreed to acquire the remaining 49% stake it does not own in engineering services provider iFocus from its shareholders, Ng Choon Tiong and Kuo Wen Siang, in two tranches for a total consideration of SG$2.9 million.

data.title: HD Hyundai Heavy Industries Raised to Buy From Neutral by Nomura >329180.SE
Australia's Green Technology Metals slumps on discounted entitlement offer
data.description:
** Shares of Green Technology Metals fall 34.5% to a record low of A$0.038

** Lithium explorer launches entitlement offer to raise about A$4 million ($2.52 million)

** Issue price of A$0.004 per new share for every 3.85 shares held represents a 31% discount to co's last close

** Says funds raised through the offer to be used for development activities at the Seymour project in Canada, among others

** 3.1 mln shares change hands, 25.5x the 30-day average of 122,677

** Stock down ~45% YTD, including current session's moves

($1 = 1.5898 Australian dollars)

data.title: Update: Over 400 Paraburdoo Workers Sign Petition Asking Rio Tinto for Collective Agreement on Pay Hike
data.description:
(Updates to add Rio Tinto's comments in the fourth and fifth paragraphs.)

More than 400 workers at Rio Tinto Group's Paraburdoo operations signed a petition for a collective agreement asking the mining giant to raise their pay, among other demands.

The Western Mine Workers Alliance (WMWA), a joint venture of the Mining and Energy Union and the Australian Workers Union, said in a Facebook post on Thursday that it will soon make a formal application to the Fair Work Commission to make an order for Rio Tinto to collectively bargain with its workforce at Paraburdoo.

The WMWA launched the majority petition early this month. Its demands include guaranteed pay increases, pay equity, and a fair classification structure amid the rising cost of living.

In an emailed statement to MT Newswires, a Rio Tinto spokesperson said the company's "existing approach helps drive productivity and wages growth."

"This model has delivered for our people, our business and the Australian economy, through the creation of jobs, strong and sustained wage growth, and the payment of royalties," the spokesperson said. Rio Tinto's statement also noted that the company will abide by Fair Work Commission requirements if the union requests to bargain.

The mining giant's shares were up over 1% in recent Friday trade.

data.title: Muthoot Finance Crosses 1 Trln Rupees Gold Loan AUM
data.description:
Muthoot Finance Ltd :

  • CROSSES 1 TRLN RUPEES GOLD LOAN AUM

data.title: Tianfeng Securities to Raise 4 Billion Yuan From Share Issuance; Shares Up 3%
data.description:
Tianfeng Securities expects to raise 4 billion yuan from the issuance of shares, according to a Thursday filing with the Shenzhen Stock Exchange.

About 1.50 billion shares will be issued to up to 35 investors, the Chinese securities broker said.

The proceeds will increase the company's capital, supplement its operating funds, and pay debts.

The issuance will not change the controlling shareholder of the company.

The company's shares rose less than 3% in recent trade.

data.title: EHang Returns to Profit in 2024
data.description:
Nasdaq-listed EHang Holdings returned to profitability after posting an adjusted net income of 43.1 million yuan in 2024, the company said in a Tuesday press release.

The Chinese autonomous aerial vehicle manufacturer recorded a year-ago net loss of 138.8 million yuan.

Total revenue surged 289% year on year to 456.2 million yuan from 117.4 million yuan, the filing said.

Moreover, the company sold and delivered 216 units in the EH216 series, soaring 315% from 52 units in the previous year.

data.title: Australia's Data#3 slips on ex-dividend trade
data.description:
** Shares of Data#3 fall as much as 4.4% to A$6.98, their biggest intraday loss since December 17, 2024

** Stock hits its lowest level since February 5

** DTL falls on ex-dividend trade, IT services co set to pay an interim dividend of 13.10 Australian cents per share

** Stock up 13% this year, including the day's move

data.title: Anka India to Acquire Futech Internet
data.description:
IT services company Anka India has agreed to acquire a 100% stake in Futech Internet to foray into the advertising industry, according to a Thursday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

The company will acquire 10,000 equity shares of face value of 10 Indian rupees from the shareholders of Futech and will also allot up to 36,154,529 equity shares to shareholders of the target.

On completion of the transaction, Futech will become a wholly-owned subsidiary of Anka India.

data.title: Sembcorp Industries Sees GSA Terminated; Shares Down 3%
data.description:
Sembcorp Industries said that its Gas Sales Agreement (GSA) signed by its wholly owned subsidiary, Sembcorp Gas, with West Natuna Exploration, Empyrean Energy and Coro Energy Duyung, was terminated.

The GSA was subject to regulatory approvals in Indonesia, which were not obtained, according to a filing with the Singapore Exchange on Thursday.

Following the termination of the deal, the company will continue to utilize its network of natural gas resources to meet demands.

Shares of the company were down over 3% in recent trading.

data.title: Gorilla Gold Mines Widens Loss in Fiscal H1; Shares Up 4%
data.description:
Gorilla Gold Mines reported Thursday that its fiscal half-year loss widened to AU$0.0058 per diluted share, from AU$0.0049 per diluted share a year earlier.

Other income for the six months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was AU$97,244, compared with AU$103,128 a year earlier.

The mining company's shares were up 4% in recent trade.

data.title: XRP Price Hints At Symmetrical Triangle, But A Crash Could Come Before The Surge
data.description:
Crypto analyst CW23 has revealed that the XRP price is hinting at a symmetrical triangle, which provides a bullish outlook for the crypto. However, he warned that a price crash could occur before XRP breaks out of this triangle and rallies to the upside. 

XRP Price Hints At Symmetrical Triangle 

In a TradingView post, CW23 revealed that a symmetrical triangle may be forming for the XRP price. This came as he noted that XRP is bouncing off the bottom of an ascending channel, with the pinbar candle on the 4-hour chart now in upward pressure having tested the bottom. The analyst added that the crypto will probably trade in this range for a while. 

However, CW23 stated that a symmetrical triangle is forming and it could be a larger wave 4 pullback in the Elliot wave before wave 5 takes the XRP price to a new all-time high (ATH) to round out this year’s bull cycle. The analyst’s accompanying chart showed that XRP could drop to as low as $1.70 on wave 4. Meanwhile, the asset is projected to rally to a new ATH of $5 on wave 5. 

Crypto analyst ElmoX also recently predicted that the XRP price could witness a massive corrective and drop below $2 before it rallies to new highs. The analyst provided a more bullish outlook for XRP, predicting it could rally to as high as $20 in this market cycle. However, he warned that the crypto would face major resistance at $2.9 on its way to a new ATH. 

The Altcoin Has Finalized Its Correction

In an X post, crypto analyst Dark Defender stated that the XRP price has finalized the correction on the four-hour time frame. He mentioned that XRP is expected to move towards $2.42 first considering the correction structures. The analyst also affirmed that the real move to the upside will start after XRP climbs above the Ichimoku clouds. 

Dark Defender highlighted $2.22 and $2.04 as the support levels to watch out for while he stated that $4.2932 and $5.8563 are the targets which the XRP price could rally to. Crypto analyst CasiTrades highlighted the importance of the altcoin holding the support levels at $2.04 and $2.11. 

She added that a hold above either of these levels is critical to maintaining the consolidation. Meanwhile, the analyst revealed that the next resistance levels are $2.25 and $2.70, which happens to be the next breakout level. CasiTrades also assured market participants that the price is still bullish, indicating it is still well primed to reach new highs. 

At the time of writing, the XRP price is trading at around $2.24, up over 2% in the last 24 hours, according to data from CoinMarketCap.

data.title: Australia's CAR Group tumbles on ex-dividend trade
data.description:
** Shares of CAR Group drop 3.2% to A$33.05, their lowest levels since August 9, 2024

** CAR stock is top loser in the S&P/ASX 200 benchmark index

** Stock trades ex-dividend

** The auto-listings firm had announced an interim dividend of 38.5 Australian cents apiece last month

** CAR stock down 8.3% YTD, including the day's move

data.title: Global Energy Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy markets. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0334 GMT - OPEC's decision to go ahead with its planned production hikes in April implies the oil market is entering a new phase where it lets underlying fundamentals drive prices, but the opposite might be true, says Daniel Hynes, senior commodity strategist at ANZ. Political considerations may also have been behind the decision, he adds. President Trump hasn't been shy in asking OPEC to help keep oil prices lower. Any rise in OPEC supply could also help the U.S. put pressure on Iran and Venezuela, Hynes adds. The Trump administration is also keen to curb Iran's nuclear program, with a maximum pressure strategy likely to significantly impact the latter's oil-generating revenue, he says. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

0258 GMT - RBC Capital Markets analysts say they are sanguine on the Australian mining sector. While commodities have been buffeted by tariff threats and soft economic data, fears of a major global slowdown are overdone, the analysts reckon. They revise their commodity forecasts, boosting their price estimates for gold but paring for metallurgical coal and lithium. "We forecast most commodity prices to be flattish/small declines in 2025," with the exception of precious metals, they say. RBC's key stock picks are Fortescue, Sandfire, Pilbara Minerals and IGO as well as Bellevue Gold, Westgold Resources, Regis Resources, South32, Mineral Resources and BHP. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

0203 GMT - Gold Circuit Electronics' business outlook appears positive, thanks to strong revenue prospects for application-specific integrated circuit AI servers and specifications upgrade trends, Daiwa Capital Markets' Sheng Cheng says in a research report. The total addressable market for ASIC AI servers' printed circuit boards is expected to reach NT$20 billion-NT$25 billion in 2025 and the Taiwanese company is likely to have a 25%-30% market share, the analyst estimates. This would probably contribute NT$5 billion-NT$6 billion in revenue for the PCB manufacturer. The brokerage raises the stock's target price to NT$290.00 from NT$270.00 and maintains a buy rating. Shares are 0.2% higher at NT$217.50. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0157 GMT - The outlook for Malaysia's utilities sector remains positive, driven by rising electricity demand, data-center expansion, and strong renewable-energy growth, RHB IB analysts Sean Lim and Miza Izaimi say in a note. Tenaga Nasional guided for steady progress in data-center projects and inquiries despite U.S. chip export restrictions, as most projects are non-AI related and primarily backed by China or Chinese-backed entities, they note. Solar power growth also remains robust, with large-scale projects rolling out, boosting contractors' order books, they add. RHB maintains an overweight rating on Malaysia's utilities sector, pegging Tenaga Nasional, YTL Power International and Solarvest as its top picks. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0157 GMT - The outlook for Malaysia's utilities sector remains positive, driven by rising electricity demand, data-center expansion, and strong renewable-energy growth, RHB IB analysts Sean Lim and Miza Izaimi say in a note. Tenaga Nasional guided for steady progress in data-center projects and inquiries despite U.S. chip export restrictions, as most projects are non-AI related and primarily backed by China or Chinese-backed entities, they note. Solar power growth also remains robust, with large-scale projects rolling out, boosting contractors' order books, they add. RHB maintains an overweight rating on Malaysia's utilities sector, pegging Tenaga Nasional, YTL Power International and Solarvest as its top picks. (yingxian.wong@wsj.com)

0016 GMT - Oil rises on a possible technical recovery after oil futures dropped moderately overnight. However, oil-price gains are likely to be capped by growing fears of a global trade war that could damp economic growth in the U.S. and other countries. "It is worth noting that oil demand growth is already anaemic and our view on the U.S. economy is consistent with demand stagnating or contracting slightly in 2025," says Kieran Tompkins, senior climate and commodities economist at Capital Economics, in commentary. Front-month WTI crude oil futures are 0.3% higher at $66.73/bbl; front-month Brent crude oil futures are 0.2% higher at $70.04/bbl. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2357 GMT - Macquarie upgrades a string of Australian mining stocks, citing a more-positive view on gold, iron ore and lithium-rich spodumene prices. It also downgrades a few--all coal-mining stocks, with the thermal-coal outlook having especially soured, according to Macquarie analysts. In a note, the analysts say South32 and BHP are Macquarie's top picks among Australia's diversified miners, and are preferred over Fortescue and Rio Tinto. Newmont is its large-cap gold pick and IGO its top bet for lithium, while Mineral Resources "offers high reward for risk," they say. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2334 GMT - While Boss Energy is raising its exposure to TSX-listed junior Laramide Resources at a relatively low cost, UBS analysts say they'd prefer the Australian uranium miner's focus remain on its flagship Honeymoon project. In a note, the analysts say they understand Boss's intentions in buying more of Laramide but remain cautious given a moratorium on uranium mining in Australia's Queensland state, where Laramide is developing the Westmoreland project. Boss Thursday said it agreed to buy 9% more of Laramide, to take its stake to more than 18%. Laramide "holds the view that uranium mining will again be allowed within the state in short order," say the analysts, while highlighting that uranium has not been produced in Queensland since 1982. UBS has a buy rating and A$3.20 target on Boss. The stock is down 0.5% at A$2.20. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2209 GMT - AGL Energy loses a bear in Barrenjoey following its review of default retail electricity market offers for FY 2026 in several states. Barrenjoey raises AGL to neutral, from underweight, as the offers suggest a better-than-expected outcome. "Our AGL forecasts in FY 2026 are now 5% above consensus EPS, and we suspect the support for greater cost recovery in non-Victorian states (and improved margin outlook) should support sentiment through to FY26 profit guidance in August," analyst Dale Koenders says. Still, Barrenjoey remains concerned about the outlook for AGL's earnings in FY 2028 when gas and coal contracts reset. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

2205 GMT - Jefferies approves of uranium miner Boss Energy's M&A ambitions. Boss Energy has bought another 9% of Laramide Resources, taking its stake in the owner of the undeveloped Westmoreland uranium project in Australia to 18.4%. That gives Boss a blocking stake on Westmoreland, and another project in the U.S., plus potential upside if they are developed, analyst Daniel Roden says. Acquiring the stake in Laramide follows previous deals with Eclipse Metals and enCore Energy. "Current transactions appear to be rational, modest and value accretive, while Honeymoon appears to be ramping up well," says Jefferies, referring to Boss's flagship uranium mine in South Australia. It retains a buy call on Boss. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

2135 GMT - Australia's S&P/ASX 200 is ready to start the day in the red after another rough session on Wall Street, where tariff threats continue to jolt markets. ASX futures are down by roughly 0.2% ahead of the open, suggesting the benchmark index will add to three consecutive days of losses. The ASX 200 ended Thursday 0.5% lower, at its lowest level since August. Gold futures notched a record high overnight, a tailwind for Australia's gold-mining stocks. Iron ore also rose, while oil futures fell. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1919 GMT - Oil futures are ground down by another flare-up in the tariff saga as President Trump threatens 200% duties on European wine and other alcoholic beverages in response to the EU's retaliatory tariff on American whiskey. The tariff back-and-forth hitting equities has filtered into the oil market, along with concerns about the longer-term impact on economic growth and demand. That has coincided with talks for a ceasefire in the Russia-Ukraine war, which could lead to additional Russian oil on the market. The IEA cited trade tensions in cutting its 2025 oil demand growth forecast to 1.03 million barrels a day from 1.1 million b/d. WTI settles down 1.7% at $66.55 a barrel, and Brent falls 1.5% to $69.88 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Geopolitics Likely Driving OPEC's Production Hike Push — Market Talk
data.description: OPEC's decision to go ahead with its planned production hikes in April implies the oil market is entering a new phase where it lets underlying fundamentals drive prices, but the opposite might be true, says Daniel Hynes, senior commodity strategist at ANZ. Political considerations may also have been behind the decision, he adds. President Trump hasn't been shy in asking OPEC to help keep oil prices lower. Any rise in OPEC supply could also help the U.S. put pressure on Iran and Venezuela, Hynes adds. The Trump administration is also keen to curb Iran's nuclear program, with a maximum pressure strategy likely to significantly impact the latter's oil-generating revenue, he says. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

data.title: Cyclopharm Secures Five-Year US Federal Supply Schedule Deal; Shares Up 4%
data.description:
Cyclopharm secured a five-year federal supply schedule agreement with the US Veterans Health Administration, expanding Technegas' adoption within the federal healthcare system, according to a Friday filing with the Australian bourse.

Technegas is a radioactive carbon dispersion used in ventilation-perfusion scans to diagnose lung conditions, including pulmonary embolism.

The deal streamlines procurement processes for US federal agencies, including the Veterans Administration (VA), Department of Defense, and Public Health Service hospitals by eliminating individual contracts across VA's 20 regional procurement offices, the filing said.

Shares rose nearly 4% in afternoon trade Friday.

data.title: BYD’s shareholders are too cautious by half
data.description:
BYD , is getting too little credit from shareholders. Sure, the Chinese electric car maker’s stock has soared 70% over the past year. And last week buyers raced to snap up $5.6 billion of new shares the company sold in Hong Kong. Yet the $146 billion group trades at a lower multiple than its main rivals, even though its core business which accounts for the bulk of its earnings sports better sales growth and margins.

That's BYD's autos unit, whose net income could hit $19 billion in 2028, based on applying a 15% tax rate to analysts’ pre-tax earnings estimates compiled by Visible Alpha. Strip out the company's $9 billion stake in listed unit BYD Electronic and the roughly $50 billion in value HSBC analysts ascribe to the battery and semiconductor units, and investors are valuing the carmaking division at less than 5 times 2028 earnings. That's less than the 17 times multiple of a basket of peers including Tesla , Chinese EV specialists like Li Auto , fellow autos giants Toyota Motor and Volkswagen .

There are headwinds, from tariffs and other unfriendly policies overseas to a price war at home. BYD’s share of the still-growing new energy vehicle market in the People’s Republic dipped to 27% in January from 34% in 2024 amidst fierce competition, according to consultancy Automobility.

The autos business looks likely to grow its top line by an annual average of nearly 20% to 2028, three times faster than the automakers in the basket. Its pre-tax margin is forecast to be around 5 percentage points higher, hitting almost 15% at the end of the period.

Moreover, BYD is catching up fast in an area where it was lagging: assisted and autonomous driving. Last month, Wang announced BYD’s “God’s Eye” smart-driving software will be included in all models priced from 100,000 yuan ($14,000).

It could be that BYD's other businesses are prompting investors to apply a conglomerate-like discount. That would be unfair, given how integral chips and batteries are to carmaking.

So, BYD Auto warrants a much higher valuation. Tesla's multiple of roughly 40 times estimated 2028 earnings is over the top. Assume BYD’s autos unit deserves half that and it would be worth $390 billion - more than twice the entire company’s current market capitalisation. It’s time BYD's investors showed more faith.

Follow @KatrinaHamlin on X

CONTEXT NEWS

Chinese electric car maker BYD raised $5.6 billion through a sale of new Hong Kong-listed shares on March 3, according to filings.

The company is now including its “God’s Eye” advanced autonomous driving system in most of its models, including all vehicles priced at about 100,000 yuan ($13,688), according to an announcement on February 10. BYD had previously only offered such features, which enable cars to navigate highway traffic autonomously under a human driver's supervision, in models priced from $30,000.

data.title: Australia Largely Isolated From Effects of Trade War — Market Talk
data.description:
0326 GMT - U.S. steel and aluminum tariffs affect just 0.2% of Australia's goods exports, and proposed tariffs on pharmaceuticals will likely have a similarly small impact, says Adam Boyton, head of Australian economics at ANZ. Australia's trade deficit with the U.S. makes it an unlikely target for country-specific tariffs, he adds. Still, Australia's exposure to developments in the U.S. isn't necessarily through direct or indirect trade linkages. Rather, it is more likely to come through uncertainty affecting business and consumer confidence, and then business-investment and household-spending decisions, Boyton says. There isn't enough evidence in soft data to suggest downside risks to growth are crystallizing, he adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; @JamesGlynnWSJ)

0254 GMT - Iron ore rises in early Asian trading. Steel mills remain pessimistic about future market demand, but profits so far have rebounded significantly along with molten-iron production, analysts at Nanhua Futures write in a note. There is little room for further downside in prices, which are already at relatively low levels, and the market remains tight, with iron-ore port inventories destocking beyond seasonal levels and fundamentals staying strong, they say. Further upside will depend on market demand, they add. The most-traded iron-ore contract on the Dalian Commodity Exchange is 1.7% higher at 789.0 yuan a ton. (kimberley.kao@wsj.com)

0238 GMT - The Singapore dollar weakens slightly against its U.S. counterpart in the Asian session amid ongoing global trade tensions. President Trump on Thursday threatened to impose a 200% tariff on U.S. imports of wine, champagne and other alcoholic beverages from the EU. "The uncertainty of Trump's tariffs looks to be hitting risk sentiment as his policy moves are coming at a time when there are increasing concerns of a U.S. recession," Maybank analysts say in a FX Research & Strategy report. However, "We stay cautious on the USD and remain wary of any downside," the analysts add. USD/SGD is 0.1% higher at 1.3371. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0127 GMT - Asian currencies are mixed against the dollar in the morning session but may be weighed by worries over U.S. tariffs. "So far, U.S. tariffs have ensnared China, and to a lesser extent South Korea, Japan and Australia," RBC Capital Markets' Alvin T. Tan says in an email. Also, President Trump has specifically mentioned South Korea and Japan in recent complaints about other countries' "unfair" trade practices, so they are likely targets for U.S. reciprocal tariffs, the head of Asia FX Strategy says. USD/KRW is up 0.1% at 1,456.19 and USD/JPY rises 0.3% to 148.29, while AUD/USD is little changed at 0.6285. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0052 GMT - South Korea's benchmark Kospi falls 0.3% to 2565.48 in early trade, as battery, steel and internet stocks retreat. Anxiety about an escalating global trade war is weighing on sentiment. Foreign investors are net sellers. Battery maker Samsung SDI falls 3.3% after a new rights issue plan, as its bigger local peer LG Energy Solution loses 2.4%. Steelmakers Posco Holdings and Hyundai Steel slide 1.4% and 2.2%, respectively. Internet platform giant Naver retreat 2.3%. Meanwhile, biosimilar company Celltrion rises 1.5% after a share cancellation plan. USD/KRW is 0.1% higher at 1,455.30 in Seoul onshore trading. South Korea's 10-year government bond yield is up 0.7 basis point at 2.770%. (kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com)

0037 GMT - Safe-haven currencies such as the dollar may continue to benefit from lingering risk aversion during the Asian session. More headlines of President Trump's tariffs are weighing on sentiment, NAB's Rodrigo Catril says. "Adding to the risk aversion in the air, Russian President flipped the U.S.-Ukraine proposal on its head saying you can't have an 'immediate' ceasefire without first agreeing on terms," the senior FX strategist says in a commentary. A search for safe-haven has been the overnight theme, Catril adds. USD/KRW rises 0.2% to 1,456.45; AUD/USD edges 0.1% lower to 0.6279. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0005 GMT - JGBs rise in the morning Tokyo session, tracking overnight price gains in U.S. Treasurys. Both JGBs and Treasurys are likely benefiting from their safe-haven appeal amid rising global trade war fears. President Trump on Thursday unveiled another salvo in a trade war with the EU, saying he would impose a 200% tariff on U.S. imports of wine, Champagne and other alcoholic beverages from the 27-nation bloc. For today, focus may be on the Japanese Trade Union Confederation's scheduled announcement of preliminary results for annual spring wage negotiations. These wage negotiations are closely watched by economists and investors for clues on the timing of future BOJ rate increases. The 20-year JGB yield is down 3bps at 2.245%. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2348 GMT - Japanese stocks may trade rangebound as uncertainty over U.S. tariffs continues. Nikkei futures are flat at 36560 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 147.92, compared with 147.64 as of Thursday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on developments related to U.S. tariffs, as well as Japanese monetary policy ahead of the Bank of Japan's policy meeting next week. The Nikkei Stock Average fell 0.1% to 36790.03 on Thursday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

2330 GMT - The Nasdaq 100 is down 13% from its high, and the S&P 500 and S&P/ASX 200 is down 10.1%, so it could be time to reset expectations on where markets could go over the next few weeks to months, says Jessica Amir, market strategist at Moomoo Australia. The current magnitude of selling and shorting is just like it was in 2018 and 2022, and investors are yet to see company earnings expectations be materially lowered, she adds. It seems a lot of investment banks' buy-the-dip calls are fading, given no one knows what's ahead, Amir says. Huge sell orders from institutions in Nasdaq 100 stocks such as Nvidia and Tesla are evident. No one will win in this trade war, she adds. (james.glynn@wsj.com; Twitter @JamesGlynnWSJ)

2034 GMT - The tariffs that the Trump administration is imposing on Mexico's imports are likely to slow down the relocation of manufacturing operations to the country from overseas, says Ricardo Pérez Vas, a Mexico City-based director at investment bank 414 Capital. The nearshoring trend in Mexico started a few years ago as trade tensions between U.S. and China and shipping disruptions caused by the Covid-19 pandemic encouraged U.S. companies to shift supply chains closer home. "The main objective of nearshoring was to replace Asian suppliers," Pérez Vas says, adding that such a goal is now compromised. "Tariffs will reduce investments in Mexican companies that primarily export to the U.S." (luis.garcia@wsj.com; @lhvgarcia)

1930 GMT - Wall Street's tariff-related worries overcome relatively benign indicators and fuel demand for low-risk Treasurys, pushing yields down. Trump threatens the E.U. with 200% tariffs on alcoholic beverages to counter the block's announced 50% levy on American whiskey and motorcycles. Fears that trade wars could weaken economic growth prevail over slower-than-expected producer inflation and lower-than-expected weekly jobless claims. The University of Michigan preliminary consumer survey is due tomorrow. Economists polled by WSJ forecast a decline to 63.2 from 67.8. The 10-year sheds 0.041 percentage point to 4.275% and the two-year falls 0.045 p.p. to 3.949%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1658 GMT - Europe's competition watchdogs shouldn't ease up on enforcing merger regulations to create homegrown corporations that can compete with counterparts in the U.S. and China, Andreas Mundt, head of Germany's Bundeskartellamt, says at a conference in Brussels. His comments come as the European Commission is under pressure to create an investment environment that can boost the EU's economy and global competitiveness. Competition regulators in both the U.K. and EU have signalled a desire to reassess merger controls to boost growth. Mundt says he supports the idea of local entities growing into European champions, but that they should not be driven by politics. "They should not be a result of subsidies or regulation, and they should definitely not be a result of weakening merger control," he says (edith.hancock@wsj.com)

data.title: Australia's Liontown Resources rises on narrower interim loss
data.description:
** Shares of Liontown Resources rise as much as 6.9% to A$0.658, their highest levels since March 7

** Stock on track to post strongest trading session since February 27

** Liontown stock among top gainers in the ASX 200 benchmark index

** The battery minerals producer posts a narrower HY net loss after tax of A$15.06 million ($9.47 million), compared with A$31 million a year ago

** Stock up 24.8% this year, including the session's move, vs a 0.4% increase in the ASX 200 benchmark index

($1 = 1.5903 Australian dollars)

data.title: Hot Chili Posts Wider Fiscal H1 Loss
data.description:
Hot Chili reported Friday a fiscal first-half loss of AU$0.0527 per diluted share, wider compared with a loss of AU$0.0346 a year earlier.

Total income for the six months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was AU$350,031, compared with AU$173,425 a year earlier.

data.title: Silver Mines Posts Wider Fiscal H1 Loss as Revenue Declines
data.description:
Silver Mines reported Friday a fiscal first-half loss of AU$0.0013 per diluted share, wider compared with a loss of AU$0.0004 a year earlier.

Revenue for the six months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was AU$65,057, compared with AU$149,402 a year earlier.

The company's shares rose 1% in recent Friday trade.

data.title: Hitachi Energy India Raises INR25.2 Billion Through QIP Issue
data.description:
Hitachi Energy India has raised 25.2 billion Indian rupees through the qualified institutions placement of equity shares, according to a Thursday filing to the Indian stock exchanges.

The company's board on Thursday approved the allotment of 2,190,688 equity shares to eligible qualified institutional buyers at an issue price of 11,507 rupees per equity share, including a premium of 11,505 rupees per equity share.

The issue price takes into account a discount of 605.50 rupees per equity share or 5% of the floor price.

The QIP issue opened on March 10 and closed on March 13.
